_meta.purpose: Machine-readable memory log of clinical-research analytic patterns. Modeled on BioMedAgent's Redis memory cache. Read at the start of every session; append after every session that surfaces a new pattern.
_meta.maintainer: clinical-research-assistant skill, sourced from real sessions
_meta.usage: Scan trigger_patterns at session start. If any current-task pattern matches, apply the action directly without re-deriving. Append new entries with date + originating_session + trigger_patterns + lesson + action. Mark superseded entries deprecated rather than deleting.

lessons.originating_session: V3 Esophageal Cancer Disparity Analysis (Bilal Mirza, U Arizona)
trigger_patterns: any pp-gap reported on a continuous variable
lessons.lesson: An overall continuous-variable gap can be a stage-distribution (or other confounder-distribution) artefact rather than a within-stratum finding.
lessons.action: Whenever a continuous-variable pp/percent gap is reported, run within-stratum analysis on the most-relevant confounder (stage, age, histology) BEFORE publishing. If the gap disappears within strata, report it as a confounding artefact, not a finding.

lessons.lesson: Cox HR on the all-stages cohort can understate the disparity in the surgically-curable Stage I-III subset because Stage IV dilutes any access-mediated effect.
lessons.action: Report HR for both the all-stages cohort AND the mechanism-relevant subset (Stage I-III). State which is primary in the abstract.

trigger_patterns: surgical access acts in first 6 months
lessons.lesson: Schoenfeld test for race PH may be globally satisfied while time-stratified Cox reveals biphasic disparity.
lessons.action: When the research question implicates a time-bound mechanism, include time-stratified Cox in 0-6, 6-12, 12-24, and 24+ month windows even when global PH holds. Report HR per window with 95% CI.

lessons.lesson: Excluding patients with Unknown values for covariates can shift adjusted ORs materially when Unknown rate exceeds 5% (e.g., OR shifted from 0.59 complete-case to 0.75 inclusive).
lessons.action: When >5% of any covariate is Unknown, present BOTH inclusive (Unknown-as-category) and exclusive (complete-case) results. Add multiple imputation as a third sensitivity. Report all three side-by-side.

trigger_patterns: primary OR or HR in abstract
lessons.lesson: E-value (VanderWeele-Ding) quantifies the magnitude of unmeasured confounding required to explain a residual association. E-value of 2.78 (surgery OR 0.58) is strong; E-value of 1.57 (Cox HR 1.15) is modest.
lessons.action: Mandatory: report E-value (point estimate + CI bound) for every fully-adjusted residual exposure effect in the abstract and main results table. Use VanderWeele-Ding formula.

trigger_patterns: more than 5 hypothesis tests in same domain
lessons.lesson: Reporting >5 separate tests without family-wise correction risks false positives. BH-FDR can flip primary-significant findings to null after adjustment.
lessons.action: For papers with >5 hypothesis tests in the same domain, generate a master significance table at the end of analysis: Endpoint, Cohort, Adjusted estimate (95% CI), Primary P, BH-FDR Q, Bonferroni-significant Yes/No.

trigger_patterns: Hispanic or NHAPI minor-category cell counts
lessons.lesson: NCDB Data Use Agreement prohibits publication of any cell with N<11. Internal worksheets should keep raw counts; publication-facing exports must mask cells with N<11 as '<11'.
lessons.action: For any NCDB analysis, generate both a raw internal version and a DUA-compliant publication version with cells N<11 masked as '<11'. Include the DUA-compliant version as a separate Excel sheet labelled 'PUBLICATION_READY'.

promoted_to.note: NCDB DUA cell-N<11 rule, line ~634 + Step 8 expansion

lessons.lesson: Reporting binary 'late-stage' rates can obscure the actual disparity, which often lives in Stage I underrepresentation and Unknown-stage over-representation rather than Stage III/IV parity.
lessons.action: Decompose stage distribution into Stage I vs II vs III vs IV vs Unknown rather than binary late-stage. Treat Unknown-stage rate as its own outcome (workup-completeness disparity).

trigger_patterns: post-CheckMate-577 cohort with limited NHB n
lessons.lesson: Subgroup analyses with one arm <500 can yield non-significant findings simply due to insufficient power. The point estimate may be in the same direction as the primary but underpowered to detect.
lessons.action: For any subgroup analysis with one arm <500, compute power for the observed effect size before interpreting. Report findings honestly as 'trend, not significant; likely underpowered (minimum detectable gap X pp at 80% power)'.

lessons.lesson: Registry data often distinguishes 'Not recommended' (provider) from 'Patient refused' (patient). When the disparity is concentrated in 'Not recommended' with identical 'Patient refused' rates, this is strong evidence for clinician-driven mechanism.
lessons.action: When studying treatment-receipt disparities, analyze the reason-for-no-treatment field by race. Provider-side excess + identical patient-side refusal = evidence for clinician-driven mechanism. Caveat that registrar-coded reasons are not audited clinical decisions.

lessons.originating_session: V4 Esophageal Cancer Disparity Analysis (cross-cohort harmonization)
lessons.lesson: Comparing two national cancer registries (SEER, NCDB) without harmonizing inclusion criteria conflates real disparity differences with cohort-definition differences. Histology coding, first/only-primary filtering, and year-window choice all matter.
lessons.action: Apply identical inclusion criteria to both cohorts before any cross-cohort comparison: (a) ICD-O-3 site, (b) histology codes, (c) first/only-primary filter, (d) year window, (e) age, (f) race-known. Document the harmonization cascade in a CONSORT-style supplementary table.

promoted_to.note: Cross-Cohort Comparison Harmonization section (NEW, this pass)

lessons.lesson: Internal hypothesis IDs (Q-numbers, internal labels) leak into manuscript tables if the table generator dumps raw analysis sheets. JAMA tables require lettered footnotes (a, b, c), No./total No. (%) format, exact P-value rules, single-spaced 10-12pt, table grid only.
lessons.action: When building manuscript tables: (1) replace internal IDs with descriptive endpoint labels; (2) use lettered footnotes (a, b, c) tied to specific cells/headers; (3) format P values as JAMA spec (.56, .003, <.001); (4) include numerator/denominator with %; (5) put SD/IQR inline with mean/median; (6) no merged cells; (7) one piece of data per cell.

lessons.lesson: JAMA Network journals require: 2 digits past decimal (.56); 3 digits if <.01 (.003); <.001 if smaller; never P=0 or P=1; never report P alone without effect size and 95% CI.
lessons.action: Use a standard P-value formatter that enforces all four rules. Apply to every reported P in tables, results text, and abstract. Always pair with effect size + 95% CI; never use star notation alone.

lessons.originating_session: BioMedAgent methodology adaptation (Bu et al, Nat Biomed Eng 2026)
lessons.lesson: Single-shot analysis or single-shot drafting fails for complex clinical-research tasks. The BioMedAgent insight applies: break the work into Plan → Execute → Verify with explicit handoffs and self-correcting feedback.
lessons.action: Apply three-phase pipeline: Phase 1 Plan (estimand, inclusion criteria, method, sensitivity analyses pre-specified); Phase 2 Execute (script every step, save to date-stamped folder); Phase 3 Verify (numerical, distributional, clinical sanity check). If verification fails, return to Phase 1, not Phase 2.

trigger_patterns: research question framed as natural language
lessons.lesson: BioMedAgent's task classification (omics / precision medicine / ML / stats / viz) maps to clinical-research as: descriptive / inferential test / multivariable / survival / sensitivity / subgroup. Method selection is downstream of classification.
lessons.action: Before choosing a method, classify the request into one of six categories using the routing table in references/biomedagent-methodology.md. A request that spans categories ('compare groups, adjusted for X, in patients with Y') should be planned as a sequence of steps across the relevant categories, not collapsed into one model.

lessons.lesson: BioMedAgent's Redis memory cache lets it skip planning steps it has solved before. Re-deriving a known pattern wastes time and risks introducing errors. The clinical-research equivalent is this lessons-log.json.
lessons.action: At the start of every session, scan trigger_patterns in this file for matches to the current task. If a match is found, apply the entry's action directly. Skip steps that have been solved before. After the session, append new entries for new patterns.

lessons.originating_session: Bilal Mirza abstract editorial philosophy (12-principle rubric)
lessons.lesson: Coherence is the primary editing criterion for an abstract. Every element must earn its place by serving a single mechanistic or narrative arc. If a finding can be removed without weakening the story, it should be moved to the manuscript.
lessons.action: Before submitting any abstract, ask: 'is this one story or two?' If reviewers will wonder why two ideas are in the same abstract, the framing — not the science — is the problem. Demote the secondary thread to manuscript supplementary; rewrite the objective so the surviving thread is clearly the focus. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 1.

lessons.lesson: Hypothesis-falsification narratives are publication-strong. A pre-registered candidate mechanism that fails, paired with an alternative mechanism that succeeds, is the Mariathasan 2018 architecture and is preferable to either a pure positive finding or a pure negative one.
lessons.action: Structure: prior literature anchors the candidate -> we tested it -> it failed -> here's what we found instead. Falsification language must appear in three places (objective, results, conclusions) so a fast-scanning reviewer cannot miss the arc. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 2.

lessons.lesson: Calibrated language tracks epistemic standing. Null results with N<100 do not 'refute' — they fail to support, provide no evidence for, do not predict. Cross-sectional associations do not 'predict treatment benefit' — they support a hypothesis-generating rationale for testing. Architecture findings in stratified analyses describe phenotypes enriched in sub-cohorts, not phenotypes characteristic of populations.
lessons.action: Flag-list of verbs/phrases that trigger re-examination: 'is refuted', 'is characterized by', 'independent of', 'predicts', 'drives', 'establishes'. They may be earned, but they often aren't. The fix is rarely deletion; usually a hedge, a cohort qualifier, or a verb downgrade. Add cohort qualifier ('in this cohort,' 'in the Asian sub-cohort') to preserve accuracy without losing impact. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 3.

trigger_patterns: TCGA / SEER / NCDB / GENIE cohort by race
lessons.lesson: Race terminology must match what was actually measured. Use the labels the source dataset used (TCGA -> Asian/White/Black; SEER/NCDB -> NHB/NHW/Hispanic/NHAPI). Do not promote self-reported race to genetic-ancestry vocabulary unless germline SNPs were measured.
lessons.action: Add 'self-reported race per registry coding' once in Methods to preempt the reviewer question (Carrot-Zhang 2020 framework). Avoid 'Caucasian' (deprecated), 'Oriental' (offensive), and ancestry abbreviations without ancestry data. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 4.

trigger_patterns: Methods section approaching size of Results
Conclusions summarizes results rather than interpreting them
lessons.lesson: Section weight signals priority. Results should be the largest section by character count — at least twice Methods and twice Conclusions. Methods compresses to the minimum that establishes rigor. Objective frames the question and prior hypothesis. Conclusions states what findings mean and what comes next, not a recap.
lessons.action: If Methods or Conclusions approaches the size of Results, the abstract is mis-weighted. Trim Methods to its rigor essentials (pre-registration, FDR control, key analytic move). Re-write Conclusions as interpretation, not recap. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 6.

trigger_patterns: draft says 'predicts preferential benefit from drug X'
cross-sectional transcriptomic data linked to therapy
lessons.lesson: Therapeutic implications must be claimed at the level the data supports. Cross-sectional transcriptomic correlations support trial-design rationale, not treatment-response prediction.
lessons.action: Use 'supports the rationale for testing X' or 'provides a hypothesis-generating rationale for evaluating X' — never 'predicts preferential benefit from X' unless a treatment-response analysis was performed. Standard test: would I be embarrassed at the podium if a discussant asked which patients in my dataset received this therapy? See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 7.

lessons.lesson: Honesty over impact when they conflict. If a softening edit costs perceived impact, take the softening. Editorial momentum tends to push abstracts toward over-claiming; the discipline is pushing back.
lessons.action: Flag-phrases for re-examination: 'is refuted', 'is characterized by', 'independent of', 'predicts', 'drives', 'establishes', 'demonstrates', 'proves'. The fix is rarely deletion; usually a hedge, a cohort qualifier, or a verb downgrade. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 9.

trigger_patterns: claim being elevated from supplementary to abstract
lessons.lesson: Iterate against the four-criterion gate before claims become abstract-grade: (a) BH-FDR q<0.05, (b) bootstrap BCa 95% CI excludes null, (c) permutation p<0.05, (d) jackknife sign-stable across leave-one-out resamples.
lessons.action: Findings that are directional or pass only nominal FDR (q<0.10) belong in supplementary, not the abstract conclusion. The therapeutic-implication sentence should be anchored on findings that cleared the gate, not on borderline results. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 11.

lessons.lesson: Compliance is non-negotiable and checked last. Character count, four-section bolded headers, absolute numbers alongside percentages, no institutional or product names in body, no abbreviations outside accepted list without first-use spell-out, generic drug names — these are absolute. Confirm character count after every revision.
lessons.action: Before declaring an abstract submission-ready, run the 12-point gate explicitly (write-abstract/SKILL.md). Output a structured table noting Pass/Fail and suggested fix for each principle. Do not declare submission-ready until each row is either pass or has an explicit accepted exception. Most-impactful priorities: 3, 7, 9 for active editing; 1, 2, 6 for narrative architecture; 10 for compliance; 11 for rigor.

promoted_to.note: 12-point gate — entire skill built around this

lessons.originating_session: Bilal Mirza standing rule — every analysis must end with a structured report
trigger_patterns: session ending with results but no report
manuscript Methods being drafted without an upstream report
future session re-deriving analyses already done
lessons.lesson: Every analysis must end with a date-stamped, structured markdown report. The report is the durable deliverable that future sessions, co-authors, and reviewers can audit. Without it the analysis is incomplete: there is no single source of truth for what was done, the manuscript Methods get re-derived from chat history, and reviewers cannot trace numbers to their generating script.
lessons.action: Generate a markdown report at the end of every analysis session. Filename pattern: analysis_report_<short-question-slug>_YYYY-MM-DD.md. Save to the project's Analyses/ or Reports/ folder. Required sections (in order, no omissions): header, research question, estimand, data sources, cohort selection cascade, variables, statistical methods, pre-specified sensitivity analyses, results (cohort characteristics + primary + secondary + sensitivity + subgroup + multiple-testing master), diagnostic checks, findings summary in plain language, limitations, reproducibility checklist, files referenced cross-walk, verification trail, next steps. Every claim includes effect size + 95% CI + P value + BH-FDR Q where applicable. Every percentage paired with numerator/denominator. Re-generate when the analysis is re-run; archive prior versions rather than overwriting. Full template lives in analyze/SKILL.md Step 9.

lessons.originating_session: HNSCC-TAM Multi-Cohort Validation (Bilal Mirza, U Arizona) — pre-submission rigor pass
trigger_patterns: any manuscript with >5 cohorts or >5 statistical claims
lessons.lesson: Before drafting a manuscript from already-completed analyses, run a structured 5-agent self-audit (numerical, statistical, biological-plausibility, code-reproducibility, completeness). The HNSCC-TAM session caught 4 critical issues — wrong AUROC value, pseudoreplication in cross-compartment correlations, an N/N validated overclaim, and missing random seeds — none of which were detectable from the report itself without comparing claimed numbers against source CSVs and re-deriving p-values at the proper sample size.
lessons.action: Whenever an analysis is being staged for manuscript drafting, run a 5-agent self-audit producing AUDIT_REPORT.md with severity-graded findings (CRITICAL / HIGH / MODERATE / MINOR). Each finding cites the source CSV row and the claimed value. Do not begin manuscript drafting until critical issues are resolved or explicitly deferred with a documented gating plan.

trigger_patterns: manuscript drafting blocked on rigor issues
user wants to systematically address audit findings
BCa CIs and BH-FDR not yet applied
lessons.lesson: A 6-phase remediation sequence cleanly closes most audit issues without requiring a full pipeline re-run: (1) surgical text/numerical fixes against source CSVs, (2) bootstrap BCa 95% CIs + BH-FDR + Bonferroni multiple-testing correction, (3) patient-level cross-compartment re-derivation + drop-LOO sensitivity, (4) canonical meta-validation table consolidating all evidence, (5) mandatory 16-section analysis report, (6) numerical re-audit cross-walking all claims against source CSVs.
lessons.action: Set up phase-numbered remediation folders (phase1_remediation/, phase2_remediation/, ..., phase6_audit/) under the project Analysis/ root. Each phase produces (a) one Python script in scripts/, (b) machine-readable outputs in results/, and (c) one PHASE*_REPORT.md narrative. Phase 6 must be a programmatic re-audit producing a PASS/FAIL log — target 100% PASS at 3-decimal precision before manuscript submission.

trigger_patterns: paired pre/post scRNA cohort with small n_patients
claim of n=8 with 4 patients × 2 timepoints
Spearman rho with degrees of freedom > true patient n
lessons.lesson: When a small discovery cohort (e.g., n=4 patients with paired pre/post sampling) is analyzed by treating each (patient, treatment) pair as an independent observation, the resulting n=8 correlations are pseudoreplicated and overstate statistical certainty. The proper patient-level analysis is n=4 (averaging pre+post within patient or using pre-only). At n=4 the minimum two-sided EXACT Spearman p-value is 2/24 = 0.083 — a structural floor, NOT a property of the data — so no patient-level n=4 correlation can reach p<0.05 by exact testing.
lessons.action: For any correlation in a small discovery cohort with paired timepoints, derive THREE framings: (a) pseudorep n_obs (sanity check vs original number), (b) patient-level n_patients (averaged or pre-only — addresses pseudoreplication), (c) pre-treatment baseline only. Use scipy.stats.permutation_test with permutation_type='pairings' for n<=5 (full enumeration) or n_resamples=10_000 for n=6-8 (Monte Carlo). The asymptotic Spearman p-value formula breaks at |rho|=1 (returns p=0 spuriously); always use exact / Monte Carlo for small n. Reframe the patient-level finding as HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING in the manuscript.

trigger_patterns: any subtype analysis where N cells >> N patients
lessons.lesson: In small scRNA-seq discovery cohorts, a single patient can dominate a cell-type pool. In the HNSCC-TAM session, Patient_2 contributed 96.66% of all TREM2+ TAM cells (984 of 1,018). Every TREM2+ TAM downstream finding (DEGs, TF activity, metabolic programs, ligand-receptor scores) is mathematically a Patient_2 finding. The dramatic 0.32x post-treatment fold change attenuated to 0.44x with Patient_2 dropped, and the Wilcoxon p-value remained non-significant in either framing.
lessons.action: For every cell-type / subtype analysis in a discovery cohort with n_patients <= 6, compute per-patient cell-pool contribution (n_cells_patient_X / n_cells_total). If any patient contributes >50% of any subtype, generate an automatic Drop-Patient-X leave-one-out sensitivity analysis covering: (a) per-patient mean proportion with vs without that patient, (b) Pre/Post fold change with vs without, (c) Wilcoxon paired test in each framing. Report both versions in the manuscript and reframe the finding as HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING if the magnitude is patient-dependent.

promoted_to.note: Small-Cohort / Single-Cell Discovery Diagnostics (NEW, this pass)

trigger_patterns: AUROC reported in clinical research manuscript
lessons.lesson: Reviewers of biomarker-prediction manuscripts routinely flag 'AUROC without 95% CI' as a critical methodological gap. Point estimates without uncertainty bands are not interpretable, especially at small N. Paired bootstrap with bias-correction-and-acceleration (BCa) 95% CI is the rigorous gold standard. At n=12 (TJU nivolumab stratum), some BCa CIs spanned [0, 1] — reflecting the true uncertainty.
lessons.action: Compute paired bootstrap BCa 95% CI on EVERY AUROC reported in the manuscript. Use 500-1000 paired (scores, labels) resamples with random_state=42; skip resamples that lose class diversity. BCa requires bias-correction z0 (from boot empirical distribution) and acceleration a (from jackknife). scipy.stats.bootstrap with method='BCa' and paired=True works; or implement manually with norm.ppf. Report as 'AUROC = X [95% BCa CI, low, high]' uniformly. Flag CIs that span 0.5 as 'not statistically distinguishable from chance.'

trigger_patterns: panel of N signatures tested in same cohort
any signature batch >= 5 in single cohort
lessons.lesson: When >=5 signatures are tested against the same outcome in the same cohort, family-wise testing inflates false positives. In GSE159067 (n=102, 14 signatures), three findings survived BH-FDR (CXCL9/10, CYT, CXCL9/CD8A) but only ONE survived Bonferroni (CXCL9/10 at alpha=0.05/14=0.0036). The Bonferroni-survivor is the load-bearing claim; BH-FDR-only survivors should be framed as 'supportive of, not independent confirmation of' the Bonferroni-survivor.
lessons.action: For every (cohort × signature panel) batch >= 5 tests, apply Benjamini-Hochberg FDR using statsmodels.stats.multitest.multipletests(method='fdr_bh'). For the largest validation cohort (typically the manuscript's anchor), additionally apply Bonferroni at alpha = 0.05/n_tests. Report BH_q and Bonferroni_significant columns alongside raw P. Frame manuscript claims by tier: Bonferroni-survivor = lead finding; BH-FDR-only = supporting; raw-P-only = hypothesis-generating.

trigger_patterns: any stochastic call without explicit seed
lessons.lesson: Without random_state=42, scanpy's sc.tl.pca / umap / leiden / neighbors / diffmap calls produce non-deterministic results. Cluster identity may be preserved across runs (Leiden is deterministic given seeded igraph + identical input) but cluster *labels* permute, which can scramble downstream analyses.
lessons.action: Add a seeded helper `seed_all.py` to every project that imports os, random, numpy, scanpy, torch and sets seed=42 across all of them. At the top of every analysis script, call seed_everything(42) before any stochastic operation. ALSO pass random_state=42 explicitly to every: sc.tl.pca, sc.tl.umap, sc.tl.leiden, sc.tl.tsne, sc.tl.diffmap, sc.tl.dpt, sc.pp.neighbors, sklearn.model_selection.train_test_split, sklearn.cluster.* . Use np.random.default_rng(42) instead of np.random.* for new code. Document this in CLAUDE.md as a project-wide rule.

promoted_to.note: Phase 2 — random_state=42 for all stochastic scanpy calls

lessons.lesson: After audit-driven rigor remediation, the manuscript's Table 1 should be a CANONICAL meta-validation table consolidating: (a) v1-corrected effect sizes, (b) bootstrap 95% CIs, (c) raw P-values, (d) BH-FDR q-values, (e) Bonferroni status, (f) status grading (ROBUST / PARTIAL / NOVEL / HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING / EXTERNALLY VALIDATED), (g) per-finding source-CSV provenance.
lessons.action: Build a Phase 4 consolidation script `phase4_build_meta_validation_v2.py` that reads all upstream CSVs (phase1_corrections, phase2_aurocs, phase3_xc_correlations) and produces: meta_validation_v2_long.csv (one row per Finding × Cohort × Statistic with full provenance), meta_validation_v2_summary.csv (one row per Finding with audit-correct headline), and meta_validation_v2.xlsx (formatted workbook). Headline-selection priority: EXTERNALLY VALIDATED > HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING (audit-corrected) > PARTIAL > ROBUST > NOVEL > largest-N fallback.

trigger_patterns: deciding what goes in abstract vs body vs discussion
lessons.lesson: After rigor remediation, partition every finding into 4 tiers for placement in the manuscript: TIER 1 (ROBUST + Bonferroni-survivor) leads the abstract and primary results; TIER 2 (PARTIAL = BH-FDR-only) appears as supportive in the body; TIER 3 (NOVEL single-cohort + EXTERNALLY VALIDATED cross-compartment) appears in main text + future-work hooks; TIER 4 (HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING) appears in Discussion + Limitations only — NEVER in the abstract or primary tables.
lessons.action: When drafting the manuscript, apply tier-based placement: (1) Abstract leads with Tier 1 findings + effect sizes + 95% CIs; (2) Results sections present Tiers 1-3 in the body with explicit multiple-testing flags; (3) Discussion §Limitations explicitly addresses Tier 4 as 'directionally consistent but not statistically distinguishable from chance after multiple-testing correction at this sample size.' This pre-empts reviewer critique of overclaiming.

lessons.lesson: Manual claim-by-claim verification of a multi-cohort manuscript is error-prone. A programmatic re-audit script that cross-walks every numerical claim in the manuscript against its source CSV at 3-decimal precision is the durable verification artifact.
lessons.action: Build a Phase 6 script `phase6_numerical_reaudit.py` that adds named checks via add_check(name, claimed, observed_in_source, tolerance, unit) and writes phase6_audit_log.csv + phase6_audit_summary.txt. Cover at minimum: (a) claimed text values vs source CSV cells, (b) summary-table values vs upstream long-table values, (c) document-internal stale-string scans, (d) sample-size sums. Target 100% PASS before submission. Produce AUDIT_REPORT_v2.md as the closure document.

trigger_patterns: PIs need a single brief vs full report
lessons.lesson: Multi-cohort manuscript projects accumulate multiple narrative documents. A clear hierarchy prevents confusion: (a) `Reports/insights_report_v*.md` = working insights document with full numerical detail; (b) `Reports/analysis_report_<question-slug>_<date>.md` = mandatory 16-section technical analysis report (per working-rules.md); (c) `Reports/FINAL_manuscript_brief_<date>.md` = manuscript-ready narrative brief consolidating story arc + cohort contributions + tier-graded evidence (this is the document PIs read first); (d) `Manuscripts/manuscript_complete_<date>.docx` = the actual manuscript deliverable.
lessons.action: Whenever a manuscript project exits the rigor remediation phases, produce the FINAL manuscript brief BEFORE drafting the manuscript itself. The brief contains: §1 Executive summary, §2 Why this project exists, §3 What we did chronologically, §4 Cohort-by-cohort contribution, §5 Four-tier evidence base, §6 Audit story, §7 Manuscript story arc with 3 title options, §8 Suggested abstract, §9 Section-by-section content map, §10 Path to submission, §11 Document index, §12 One-sentence elevator pitch. PIs review the brief first to confirm direction, then the manuscript drafts against the brief.

lessons.originating_session: Esophageal-IO V4 — race × era interaction audit (Bilal Mirza, U Arizona)
trigger_patterns: audit sub-question contains 'race × X' or any A:B interaction
interpretation prose substitutes one outcome's interaction P for another
deciding whether to report formal interaction tests in main text vs supplement
manuscript has comparator paper in same subfield
lessons.lesson: Two intertwined disciplines that the V4 audit had to learn the hard way. (1) AUDIT TAGGING: every audit step that involves an A × B interaction must be tagged with both `outcome` and `dataset` fields, and the audit-report prose must repeat that tagging. A P value computed on one outcome (e.g., surgery receipt) must NEVER be interpreted in the audit report as if it spoke to a different outcome (e.g., overall survival), even when the conceptual interaction question is the same. Cross-check the audit's Phase-1 gap list against the manuscript abstract's headline claims one-by-one before declaring the audit complete. (2) COMPARATOR-ALIGNED REPORTING: when choosing whether to report a formal interaction test in the manuscript main text, the most defensible default is to match the comparator paper's reporting practice in the same subfield and same journal tier. For disparity research published in JCO / Lancet Oncology / JAMA Oncology, the field-standard pattern (e.g., Miller 2024 Lancet Oncol on VHA ICI effectiveness by race) is descriptive within-subgroup analyses without formal interaction P values. Match that pattern; run the formal interaction test internally as an audit sensitivity so it is in the back pocket if reviewers demand it; report it only in supplementary or a reviewer-response packet.
lessons.action: When auditing a multi-outcome manuscript, tag every interaction-test entry with `outcome` and `dataset` fields. Run the formal interaction test for every claim that depends on effect modification, even if you do not plan to report it in main text — the result is needed for reviewer-response readiness. Before deciding what to report in main text, identify the closest published comparator paper (same subfield, same journal tier) and check whether they reported formal interaction tests; align your reporting to their practice unless your abstract makes a stronger effect-modification claim than theirs. Document the decision and the comparator citation in the audit report so future sessions do not re-derive the analysis from scratch. For Esophageal-IO V4 specifically: comparator is Miller S et al, Lancet Oncol 2024 (PMID 39551068; DOI 10.1016/S1470-2045(24)00528-X) — descriptive within-race subgroup reporting only; V4 main-text matches that practice; formal HCA-4 / HCA-9 / HCA-10 interaction results retained internally in the audit report as supplementary sensitivity.

lessons.originating_session: V4 Esophageal-IO — Miller-2024 comparator gap closure (V4_22 P1-P5)
lessons.category: Method: causal-inference rigor for within-treatment-recipient subgroup comparisons
trigger_patterns: within-recipient HR being reported in a multi-stage access-disparity manuscript
comparing observational results to an equal-access (e.g., VHA) effectiveness study
manuscript reports unmatched HR within ICI / chemo / surgery recipients
reviewer asks whether the within-recipient disparity persists after balancing on access factors
lessons.lesson: When a multi-stage access-disparity manuscript reports a within-treatment-recipient hazard ratio (e.g., NHB vs NHW within ICI recipients), an unmatched within-recipient HR is not a sufficient anchor — measured access factors at the moment of treatment receipt (timing from diagnosis to treatment, prior multimodal therapy, comorbidity, facility type, insurance) differ between groups, and these differences confound the within-recipient effectiveness estimate. The within-recipient HR must therefore come from a 1:1 propensity-matched analysis on those access factors, with a complementary IPTW sensitivity (logistic vs flexible-ML PS), a propensity-weighted KM figure, a histology-stratified version, and an explicit time-to-treatment Cox sensitivity. The pentet of outputs collectively determines whether the residual within-recipient disparity supports an 'access barriers operate even within recipients' framing or an 'after balancing, outcomes equalize' framing — the manuscript-language decision flips on this analysis.
lessons.action: Whenever a draft reports a within-treatment-recipient HR (or unadjusted within-recipient comparison) for an effectiveness disparity claim: (1) build a 1:1 propensity-matched cohort on the full access-factor set including timing-to-treatment; (2) prespecify the matched-HR cutoff that flips manuscript framing (e.g., HR >= 1.20 with CI excluding null -> retain access-barriers framing; HR ~ 1.0 -> rewrite to equivalence-after-matching); (3) replicate using a flexible-ML PS (gradient-boosted-tree IPTW) as a misspecification sensitivity, with a 0.10-HR-unit delta threshold for declaring concordance; (4) generate IPTW-weighted KM curves; (5) repeat the matched analysis stratified by the dominant biological subtype (e.g., EAC vs ESCC) AND test the formal interaction on the OUTCOME of interest (per L038, tag both outcome and dataset); (6) refit the matched Cox with treatment-timing as an additional covariate and report the Δ-HR explicitly, since timing is a key candidate mediator. In V4 (NCDB Stage IV ICI 2018+, NHW vs NHB) this yielded matched HR 1.25 (95% CI 1.03-1.53; P=.03), Δ(GBT-IPTW vs logistic-IPTW)=0.02 HR units, and ΔHR after time-to-ICI adjustment = +0.03 (timing accounts for none of the residual gap). The unmatched HCA-10 HR of 1.27 was therefore replaced by the matched HR of 1.25 in the V4 Discussion, retaining the access-barriers framing. See Scripts/V4_22_within_ICI_PSM_and_GBT_sensitivity.py and Section 8.10 of analysis_report_methodological_audit_2026-05-06.md.

promoted_to.note: Within-Recipient Comparison Reporting HARD RULE (NEW, this pass)

lessons.originating_session: V4 PSM caliper-sensitivity review (external reviewer note)
trigger_patterns: reviewer asks whether caliper is binding
lessons.lesson: When a PSM caliper-sensitivity table shows nearly identical matched-pair counts across several caliper widths (e.g., 0.10 / 0.20 / 0.25 × SD), the cause may be either (a) the caliper is not binding because PS overlap is excellent (a substantive robustness finding) or (b) a bug in the matching loop that fails to apply the caliper. The two are indistinguishable from the count alone; an extended diagnostic sweep is required.
lessons.action: For every PSM analysis, run an extended caliper sweep at 0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00 × pooled SD logit-PS, plus an unbounded sweep (no caliper). For each caliper, report (1) matched-pair count, (2) % of treated successfully matched, (3) median actual match distance, (4) maximum actual match distance, and (5) explicit Yes/No flag for whether the caliper is binding (max distance ≈ caliper). If the caliper is not binding for conventional widths (≥ 0.05 × SD), state this explicitly in the manuscript as a substantive robustness finding rather than as a placeholder caveat. Diagnostic script template: V4_18b_caliper_diagnostic.py.

promoted_to.note: Caliper-binding diagnostic row + interpretation (NEW, this pass)

lessons.originating_session: V4+V5 Esophageal-IO final manuscript citation audit (overnight 2026-05-06)
lessons.category: Citation verification: PubMed-grounded audit + add-time hallucination prevention
trigger_patterns: any new reference is added to a manuscript or bibliography
any bibliography entry is created or modified
user says 'add a citation', 'add a reference', 'cite this paper', or any equivalent
before any manuscript Phase 8 audit completion
any PMID, DOI, or citation appears in a manuscript draft
any time a sentence is added that ends with [N] or (Author Year)
lessons.lesson: Models can and do fabricate references. During the V4+V5 Esophageal-IO citation audit (2026-05-06), two references were confirmed fabricated and three more had wrong PMID/DOI: (1) Lautenbach DM 'Cancer Med 2023 esophageal disparities' — the DOI 10.1002/cam4.6206 resolved to a Chinese SCLC brain-mets MRI paper (PMID 37288842), the claimed paper does not exist in PubMed; (2) Chua IS 'JAMA Netw Open 2024' — PMID 39173090 and DOI were correct but every other field (authors, title, journal, volume, pages) was fabricated to match an imagined paper, real paper is Chua AV et al JCO Oncol Pract 2024;21(3):408-417; (3) NCCN Ajani Esophageal 2023 DOI was 10.6004/jnccn.2023.0020 (the NSCLC NCCN paper) instead of 10.6004/jnccn.2023.0019; (4) Boscoe FP PMID was 27006255 (a PPI paper) instead of 26991033; (5) the Methods text cited [41] and [42] but the manuscript reference list ended at [34]. The risk is highest when models add 'comparator literature' sentences in Discussion paragraphs, generate methodology citations on the fly, or fill in 'pending verification' PMIDs without verification.
lessons.action: Two non-negotiable rules: (A) Add-time verification — every new citation added to a manuscript or bibliography file must be PMID-verified against PubMed via the bio-research plugin's get_article_metadata or lookup_article_by_citation tools BEFORE the citation is written into any file. If a PMID is unknown, use lookup_article_by_citation with (journal, year, volume, first_page, author) to find the real PMID; never write 'PMID: pending verification' and move on. If the lookup returns NOT_FOUND or AMBIGUOUS, flag the citation as [VERIFY] inline and do not include claim-supporting text that depends on it. (B) Pre-submission audit — every manuscript Phase 8 audit must include a Citation Integrity Audit step that (1) extracts every PMID/DOI in the manuscript reference list AND bibliography file, (2) batch-verifies each via PubMed (DOI → paper, PMID → paper), (3) checks that returned title/authors/journal/year/volume match the citation claim, (4) checks that every inline [N] citation has a corresponding ref list entry, (5) checks for duplicate PMIDs across different ref numbers, and (6) writes a date-stamped citation_audit_YYYY-MM-DD.md report listing every check. Worked example: Active_2026-05-06/Reports/citation_audit_2026-05-06.md (Esophageal-IO V6). Specific anti-hallucination heuristics: papers cited as 'an earlier NCDB cohort comparator', 'a prior systematic review', or 'an equal-access comparator paper' have the highest fabrication risk and must be PubMed-verified before being written into any file; papers with PMIDs ending in repeating digits (37015337 vs 37015332) or shared with another already-cited paper are high-suspicion for off-by-one or stolen-PMID errors and must be re-verified.

trigger_patterns: user requests Discussion rewrite, Discussion edit, or new Discussion section
user pastes Discussion text asking for review or revision
user says 'rewrite the discussion', 'strengthen the discussion', 'shorten the discussion', or asks for a Discussion that interprets results
any manuscript Phase 8 audit step that reviews Discussion structure
lessons.lesson: The skill's prior structure (Aga & Nissar 2022 reverse-funnel) is correct but insufficient: it does not codify the rhetorical moves that make a Discussion publication-ready. The combined RPTH (Research and Practice in Thrombosis and Haemostasis, 2024) Discussion-writing guide + Miller et al. 2024 (VA equal-access ICI study, Lancet Oncol) rhetorical principles produce a tighter, more reviewer-resistant Discussion in 8 paragraphs: (1) 'The main findings of this study are…' opening with n + design + hedged 'to our knowledge' first claim; (2) most important finding compared to 2–4 prior studies by name with concrete numbered extensions; (3) NEGATIVE/null finding treated as informative and used to reframe prior literature; (4) equal-access vs non-equal-access mechanism contrast where applicable; (5) two-mechanism framing (biology vs structural-racism) with explicit 'consider structural before biological' rhetorical move borrowed verbatim from Miller; (6) implications split into methodological + clinical + future work; (7) strengths + limitations with literal 'BIAS, CONFOUNDING, CHANCE' labels and direction-of-bias commentary; (8) strong Conclusion with main takeaways and largest modifiable lever named. Forbidden in Discussion: HR/OR/CI/P values (belong in Results+Tables; percentages and effect-size descriptors permitted), em dashes as prose punctuation, self-promoting language ('striking', 'novel'), methods restatement, new findings. Required register: hedged replication-aware language throughout; explicit acknowledgement of what registry data cannot determine. Worked example: Esophageal-IO/Active_2026-05-06/Manuscripts_Final_2026-05-16/manuscript_2026-05-16_MBM.docx after Scripts/V67_rewrite_discussion_RPTH_style.py.
lessons.action: When writing any Discussion: (A) apply the 8-paragraph mandatory structure above; (B) run quality-check audit before delivery: stat-pattern grep (HR, OR, 95% CI, P =, P <, chi-square P, adjusted HR, adjusted OR, matched HR) must return zero hits in Discussion region, em-dash count must be 0, first sentence must approximate 'The main findings of this study are…', at least one paragraph must deploy two-mechanism framing with explicit 'consider structural first', limitations paragraph must contain the literal labels Confounding/Bias/Chance, Discussion must contain hedged language ('could', 'might', 'merits', 'consistent with'), at least one paragraph must explicitly discuss what was NOT found; (C) where the prior literature is mixed or null, name the discordant studies and explain the difference rather than only citing confirmatory work; (D) for any unmeasured-confounder discussion, state the direction of bias (toward or away from the null) and translate the limitation into a specific future-work recommendation. The rule is codified in three places: (1) ~/Claude/dev/clinical-research-assistant/clinical-research-assistant/skills/write-discussion/SKILL.md section 'RPTH + Miller VA Discussion Framework'; (2) ~/Claude/00_Context/working-rules.md section 'Refinement 2026-05-17 (L043) — Discussion-writing framework'; (3) this lessons-log entry. Source papers: Aga & Nissar 2022 PMC9458406 (reverse-funnel); RPTH Discussion guide PMC10772867 (8-paragraph structure); Miller et al. 2024 PMC12169339 (VA ICI equal-access; structural-before-biological rhetorical move).

promoted_to.note: RPTH/Miller framework alongside Aga & Nissar reverse-funnel

trigger_patterns: user reviews a manuscript and asks about font/spacing/colour
lessons.lesson: Every manuscript, abstract, and review-ready Word document must use Georgia 12pt, 1.5 line spacing, black (RGB 0,0,0) text, no coloured shading. This applies globally to every clinical-research project, not just any one manuscript. Pandoc-rendered docx files retain per-run font overrides from the markdown's inline formatting that override the document's Normal style; setting only the Normal style does NOT propagate to existing paragraphs and runs. To force the standard, every paragraph and every run in every table cell must be walked and reformatted explicitly. Worked example: Esophageal-IO/Active_2026-05-06/Scripts/V8_apply_georgia_formatting_2026-05-11.py.
lessons.action: When generating any manuscript or abstract .docx, run a post-processing formatter that: (1) sets Normal style font=Georgia, size=12pt, color=black, line_spacing=1.5; (2) sets w:rFonts on the Normal style for ascii, hAnsi, cs, and eastAsia all = 'Georgia'; (3) iterates every paragraph style (type == 1) and applies same font + color + line spacing; (4) walks every paragraph and every run, forcing run.font.name = 'Georgia', run.font.color.rgb = RGBColor(0,0,0), and setting w:rFonts on the run's rPr; (5) recursively does the same for every table cell paragraph. Tables may use 11pt font for fit but otherwise identical formatting. The rule is also codified in ~/Claude/00_Context/working-rules.md under 'Manuscript & Abstract Formatting Standard (GLOBAL)'.

lessons.originating_session: Esophageal Cancer Disparities — QC audit + analysis consolidation
trigger_patterns: multiple dated analysis output files (V## series)
manuscript prose contradicts its own table
stale analysis vintage in a manuscript
verify manuscript numbers against analysis files
lessons.lesson: Scattered date-stamped analysis outputs (V36/V44/V45/V47/V48/V50/V53/V55/V58 across two dates) make it impossible to know which file holds the current value of a quantity; the manuscript drifts to a stale vintage and prose contradicts its own tables (Esophageal-IO: latest V55 reproduced Table 2b within-NHB CSS HR 0.912 P=.25, NOT the prose 0.934 P=.18 — prose was the stale, unsourced vintage). 'Use the latest analysis' does not mean 'one latest file is the whole truth': V50 was the newest SEER-treatment file but had no survival/NCDB/genomic/within-ICI, so it could not be the sole source. ~9 contested quantities (2-yr CSS %, adjuvant nivolumab, Stage IV ICI uptake, TMB-High OR, genomic ESCC/MSI) were computed by NO current analysis file and must be flagged, not back-filled from the draft.
lessons.action: Maintain exactly ONE living registry per project: `<project>/Reports/MASTER_ANALYSIS_REGISTRY.json` (machine, update-in-place) + auto-rendered `MASTER_ANALYSIS_REGISTRY.md` (human). Each result entry carries `current` + a `history[]` of every superseded value IN THE SAME ENTRY (old value/source/date/superseded_by/reason) — nothing lost, no version hunting. New analyses call `analysis_registry.py upsert` (auto-supersede; never spawn a new scattered file); precedence is latest-file-PER-QUANTITY (supersede only what was recomputed). Quantities no file computes -> `flag` UNSOURCED with the re-derivation action (honesty over impact; never interpolate). On consolidation, MOVE folded outputs to `Archives/Analysis_consolidated_<date>/` (scripts NEVER archived; Rule 2), record `archived_sources[]` + `update_log[]`. Manuscript/abstract/table numeric reconciliation pulls locked values ONLY from this registry; UNSOURCED quantities are reported as blockers needing a fresh analysis or PI decision. Reusable tool: `skills/analyze/scripts/analysis_registry.py`. Full standard: working-rules.md 'Single Consolidated Analysis Registry Standard' + analyze/SKILL.md 'After Analysis'.

lessons.originating_session: Esophageal Organ-Preservation HTE Phase 1 NCDB cohort assembly (Bilal Mirza)
lessons.category: NCDB data verification: SSDI completeness + external surgery-schema anchoring
trigger_patterns: any new NCDB site-specific PUF analysis
analysis plan depends on a Site-Specific Data Item (SSDI) variable as a response or exposure construct
analysis plan depends on RSPNS_TO_NEOADJUVT, PERCNT_NECROS_POST, or any other yp-response SSDI
analysis plan uses RX_SUMM_SURG_PRIM_SITE to define a surgical arm
any cohort definition requires a site-specific 2-digit surgery code table
user says 'parse the NCDB esophagus PUF', 'build cohort from NCDB site-PUF', or any equivalent for a non-esophagus site PUF
Phase 1 cohort assembly for any NCDB site-specific analysis
lessons.lesson: Two NCDB site-PUF gotchas surfaced during Esophageal Organ-Preservation Phase 1 (2026-05-17) and cost a partial SAP amendment to recover from. Both are easy to miss and both invalidate downstream analyses if not caught pre-Phase-2. (1) SSDI completeness is site-specific and frequently zero in any given site PUF. In the esophagus 2023 PUF, RSPNS_TO_NEOADJUVT (SSDI 3922) and PERCNT_NECROS_POST (SSDI 3908) are 100% empty (0 non-missing of 23,616). Any response construct that depends on these fields will silently collapse to all-missing if not audited; for esophagus the only available response signal is post-neoadjuvant pathologic stage (AJCC_TNM_POST_PATH_T/N → ypT0N0 = pCR), which is observable only in the trimodality arm (the central design constraint). The same risk exists for grade (100% missing in the 2018–2022 esophagus extract due to the SSDI-vs-legacy split at the AJCC 8th-ed cutover) and tumor size (96% missing same cause). (2) NCDB Appendix A does not contain the site-specific RX_SUMM_SURG_PRIM_SITE 2-digit code table for many sites — it is a pointer to the external NAACCR/STORE manual, and the boundary between esophagus-preserving local destruction/excision (codes 10–27) and esophagectomy (≥30) is not in the dictionary, the SAS PROC FORMAT, or the QuickStart PDF. Anchoring the esophagectomy boundary to the public NAACCR/STORE schema is correct in principle but every arm count remains ASSUMPTION-DEPENDENT until externally verified against the current STORE manual (worked example: Esophageal Organ-Preservation Phase 1 §HALT/AMBIGUITY notice; sensitivity S1 treating code 80 'resection NOS' as unclassifiable is reported but does not replace the manual verification).
lessons.action: Every NCDB site-PUF Phase 1 analysis MUST include two explicit pre-analytic verification steps before any cohort count, Table 1, or downstream model is locked. (A) SSDI completeness audit. For every variable named in the SAP as a response, exposure, modifier, or adjustment covariate, compute non-missing-N / total-N in the analytic frame and tabulate. Flag any variable with <10% non-missing as STRUCTURALLY EMPTY (not amenable to imputation). If a SAP-named response variable is structurally empty, halt and file a dated SAP §9 amendment specifying the fallback construct (e.g., for esophagus: pCR := ypT0N0 derived from AJCC_TNM_POST_PATH_T/N, observable in trimodality arm only) BEFORE proceeding to Phase 2. Worked example: Esophageal Organ-Preservation Active_2026-05-17/notes/PROTOCOL.md §9 amendment A1 (2026-05-17), items 1–5. (B) Surgery-schema external verification. For any cohort that uses RX_SUMM_SURG_PRIM_SITE to define a surgical arm, verify the site-specific 2-digit code table against the current public NAACCR/STORE manual (esophagus = NAACCR Data Item #1290/1291) AND against SEER*RSA's site-specific surgery page, AND check the observed code distribution for corroboration. Document the boundary explicitly in the Phase 1 report with an §HALT/AMBIGUITY notice flagging that all arm counts are assumption-dependent until manual-verified. Pre-specify and report at least one sensitivity that re-classifies the ambiguous-NOS code (e.g., for esophagus code 80) as unclassifiable. Recommended action: STORE 2018/2021/2022 manual verification before locking Phase 2 — code the surgery-class function once and reference the verified manual page in a comment. Both verifications belong in the Phase 1 analysis report, in the §HALT/AMBIGUITY block at the top of the report.

lessons.originating_session: CR-POPF v1→v2→v3→v4 manuscript restructuring (Bilal Mirza). New Tables_Updated.docx introduced Table 3 (predictors of CR-POPF) — a new model direction — but v1 through v3 kept a one-direction Introduction even as Tables 1, 2, 3 and a new Predictors-of-CR-POPF Results subsection were added. The user caught the asymmetry; v4 restructured to a 5-paragraph two-pronged narrative.
lessons.category: Manuscript narrative architecture: Introduction ↔ Results structural symmetry when new analytical dimensions are added to an existing draft
trigger_patterns: a new table is added to an existing manuscript
a new Results subsection is added whose subheading is not paraphrased in the Introduction aim
a new model direction is added (e.g., outcome→exposure becomes exposure→outcome, or a predictor model is added alongside an existing outcome model)
a new co-primary endpoint or new primary endpoint is added
a sensitivity analysis is promoted to primary
user describes the study with 'two-pronged', 'dual', 'co-primary', 'in parallel', 'bidirectional', 'predictor and consequence', 'antecedent and downstream', or any similar dual-direction language
user explicitly asks 'why is this not in the introduction', 'the introduction does not mention X', or 'we have to update the introduction'
table-replacement + prose-reconciliation tasks that include new analytical dimensions (not just new numbers in old models)

the Introduction or Discussion ¶1 is restructured (re-audit the Discussion body order against the Results subsection order — restructuring ¶1 without restructuring ¶2 onward is the canonical failure mode)
the user asks why the Discussion order differs from the Introduction or Results order — treat the question itself as a symmetry violation signal
a one-direction draft is amended with a new arm — the new arm's Results subsection is present but the Discussion body retains the old one-direction sequence
lessons.lesson: The existing manuscript-qc 12-check workflow tests Methods→Results alignment (Check 3) and Introduction-objective→Results coverage (Check 12), but neither tests the inverse Results→Introduction direction. When a new analytical dimension is added to an existing manuscript, a new Results subsection can be fully populated and the Methods can correctly describe the new method, and both checks pass — while the Introduction still telegraphs only the original aim. The asymmetry is structurally invisible to existing checks because each Results subsection *does* have a Methods sentence and the *existing* aim *does* have a corresponding Result; what is missing is the *parallel aim* for the new dimension. Specifically: in the CR-POPF v1–v3 sequence, the original manuscript was framed one-directionally around 'downstream burden of CR-POPF'; the new Tables_Updated added Table 3 (predictors of CR-POPF), which is a model in the opposite direction. v1–v3 added the Methods sentence, the Results subsection, and a Discussion paragraph for the new model — but kept a one-direction Introduction. L042 (number reconciliation), citation-audit Rule B, and the 12-check QC all passed because none of them tests Introduction→Results subsection coverage from the Results side. The user caught the architectural mismatch; v4 restructured the Introduction to a 5-paragraph two-pronged narrative. | v5-refinement (2026-05-18): A second instance of the same narrative-architecture-symmetry violation was caught — the Discussion body order in v4 inverted what the Introduction and Discussion ¶1 promised (consequences in ¶3–4 before predictors in ¶5–6). This is a subtle artifact of how the manuscript evolved: the original v1 was one-directional (consequences-only), so ¶3–4 were the historical core. When the predictors arm was added in later versions, the new predictors paragraph was appended after the existing consequences paragraphs rather than placed in the architecturally correct position. v5 reorders the Discussion body to predictors-first to match the Introduction and Results sequence. The new explicit instruction in Check 13 is: Discussion body paragraph order must match Results subsection order, and any restructuring of ¶1 must trigger a re-audit of ¶2 onward.
lessons.action: Add Check 13 (Introduction ↔ Results Structural Symmetry) to dev/clinical-research-assistant/clinical-research-assistant/skills/internal/manuscript-qc/references/checks.md. The check requires: (1) enumerate every Results subsection in order; (2) for each subsection, locate the Introduction sentence(s) that motivate it (gap statement OR aim/objective); (3) flag any Results subsection whose motivating Introduction sentence is absent OR is only implicit because the framing covers only a subset of the Results; (4) apply the same symmetry to Discussion ¶1 (principal findings) — every primary Results subsection must appear; (5) apply to Abstract Introduction + Conclusions. Severity: CRITICAL if a primary aim is missing from the Introduction; MAJOR if a secondary aim or sensitivity analysis is missing. Re-run Check 13 from scratch whenever any of the trigger patterns above appear. Also update working-rules.md with the 'Narrative-Architecture Symmetry rule' so it applies session-wide before the plugin is reinstalled. Worked example: CR_POPF/Manuscript/POPF Burden_MBM 2026-05-18_v3.docx → v4.docx (v3 passed every existing QC check; v4 restructured Introduction to 5-paragraph two-pronged narrative + Discussion ¶1 rewritten to 'twofold' findings). v5 worked example: CR_POPF/Manuscript/POPF Burden_MBM 2026-05-18_v4.docx → v5.docx — v4 Discussion ¶1 said 'twofold findings: First predictors, Second consequences', but body ¶3–4 discussed consequences and body ¶5–6 discussed predictors, inverting the framing. v5 reorders body to ¶3 predictors → ¶4 prevention literature → ¶5–6 consequences → ¶7 mitigation → ¶8 limitations. The check now requires verifying that the Discussion body topic sequence is identical to the Results subsection sequence whenever the Introduction or ¶1 is restructured.

lessons.originating_session: Esophageal Organ-Preservation HTE — full Standing-Rule-A analysis (5 rungs, N=53,389 NCDB) completed before discovering Sakowitz et al. 2025 (J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg, N=3,786, NCDB SCC stage II-III) reporting essentially identical headline HR 1.75; Sakowitz was published 4 months prior to our analysis and would have been surfaced by a pre-design literature scan (Bilal Mirza)
lessons.category: Research workflow: pre-design literature recon as non-skippable gate before analysis design lock
trigger_patterns: user invokes /analyze and project lacks evidence_bank.json or citation_bank.json or novelty_assessment.json or differentiation_brief.md
user invokes /analyze and the novelty_assessment is >30 days old
user invokes /analyze and the research_question_sha256 in novelty_assessment.json no longer matches study_spec.research_question
/project-init completes and user proceeds straight to /analyze without literature recon
user describes a new study and asks 'is anyone else doing this?' or 'has this been published?' — that question signals the gate is needed
Phase 1 INTAKE about to lock specs without prior-work landscape established
post-analysis discovery of a comparable NCDB / SEER / institutional-cohort paper that overlaps with the locked design
Discussion writing surfaces a high-overlap comparator not previously known
user asks 'why did we do this if someone already did it?'
user asks 'why was the [comparable paper] not flagged earlier?'
lessons.lesson: Running /analyze without a deliberate pre-design literature scan is the canonical failure mode that produces analyses redundant with literature published in the prior 12 months. The CRA workflow as originally structured (project-init → analyze → ... → literature-review at Discussion stage) treated lit review as a write-up input rather than a study-design input. That ordering allows months of work to land on top of a comparable published paper — discovery only happens at Discussion when the manuscript is already half-drafted. Concrete instance (Esophageal Organ-Preservation HTE, 2026-05-23): a full Standing-Rule-A analysis (5 rungs — KM/RMST, multivariable Cox + Schoenfeld + interactions, time-stratified Cox, PSM/IPTW/AIPW + sensitivity bundle, subgroup HTE on pre-locked SCC+cN0 primary endpoint) was completed on N=53,389 NCDB patients before the user asked for a literature comparison, which surfaced Sakowitz et al. 2025 (J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg, N=3,786 NCDB stage II-III SCC) reporting HR 0.57 for trimodality ≡ HR 1.75 for definitive CRT — essentially identical to our overall finding (HR 1.75) and RMST diff (9.17 mo vs our 10.75 mo). The work is not wasted (the HTE quantification across all 4 histology x cN cells, the adenocarcinoma inclusion, the time-stratified Cox showing non-PH attenuation 1.75→1.38, the 4-estimator concordance, the common-support trimming for SCC+cN0, and the E-value + tipping-point sensitivity bundle are genuinely novel methodological extensions Sakowitz did not address) but the manuscript framing must now reposition from 'first NCDB evidence on this question' to 'replication with extension', a decision that should have been made at Phase 1 not at Phase 7. The structural fix is to make /literature-review a Phase 0 prerequisite that /analyze auto-invokes and gates on PI sign-off via a structured differentiation brief.

lessons.lesson: When the assistant writes synthesis or comparison prose, narrative momentum produces fabricated supporting facts even when the underlying analytic work is correct. Concrete instance (2026-05-24): the Esophageal Organ-Preservation HTE literature_comparison report incorrectly stated 'NCDB 2010-2022 (more contemporary)' for our cohort vs Sakowitz 2025's '2004-2021', presenting the time window as a differentiation point. The actual cohort spans 2004-2022 — same start year, +1 year end — making the time windows essentially identical and the 'more contemporary' framing both false and a weakening (not strengthening) of the comparison. The error was structurally invisible: the project state files (study_spec, dataset_spec, phase1_consort report, cohort.parquet) all correctly state 2004-2022; only the synthesis prose I wrote later was wrong. L041 (citation integrity) covers external citations; L042 (number reconciliation) covers manuscript numbers vs results_registry. Neither catches free-text framing claims about the cohort itself when the assistant is building a differentiation argument. The bug is: when synthesizing a 'why our study matters' argument, the assistant inserts plausible-sounding supporting facts without re-checking the data.
lessons.action: Before any synthesis / comparison / differentiation prose is locked, every numerical or temporal claim about the current study MUST be verified against project state files via the following hard-gate sequence. (1) Identify every claim in the prose that asserts a property of the current study (cohort N, year range, follow-up duration, event count, effect estimate, sample composition, methodology specifics). (2) For each claim, look up the corresponding field in study_spec.json, dataset_spec.json, variable_spec.json, results_registry.json, or read directly from data/working/cohort.parquet. (3) If the claim does not match: HALT, correct the prose to match the data, append a decision_log entry documenting the original wrong claim + correction. (4) Framing claims (like 'more contemporary', 'larger', 'first', 'unique') require a quantitative anchor (e.g., 'X years vs Y years', 'N=53,389 vs N=3,786') with both numbers verified. (5) If the framing claim cannot be supported by data, remove it — do not soften it. Promote enforcement to: (a) literature-review skill (Phase 0 Mode 0 differentiation_brief population); (b) write-introduction (study-design paragraph); (c) write-discussion (any 'our study showed' or 'we found' sentence); (d) write-manuscript Phase 8 final audit; (e) manuscript-qc as new Check 16 (claim verification: every numerical/temporal claim cross-referenced to state). Worked example: Esophageal-Organ-Preservation/Reports/literature_comparison_2026-05-24.md and the decision_log.md entry for 2026-05-24 (PI-flagged correction).

lessons.originating_session: Esophageal Organ-Preservation HTE SEER replication — NCDB Phase 1 silently defaulted 'all primaries' (no sequence-number filter applied) while SEER Phase 1 in the same project silently defaulted 'first primary only' (Sequence='One primary only' OR '1st of N or more'). The two cohorts were therefore not methodologically comparable: NCDB N=53,389 vs SEER N=13,940, with ~16,000 of the difference attributable to this single filter divergence. PI Bilal Mirza caught the mismatch and asked 'why?', exposing that the inclusion/exclusion criteria review at Phase 1 INTAKE is not gated on a structured checklist — the assistant writes down the filters it remembers and the user signs off without seeing what was never considered. This is invisible to L048 (pre-design lit recon) and L049 (synthesis claim verification) — it's a different bug, in Phase 1 itself.

lessons.lesson: At /analyze Phase 1.1 dataset_spec generation, the assistant typically writes down the filters that come to mind from the SAP, the registry conventions, or recent similar projects — and the PI signs off on what is presented. There is no enforcement that every CONVENTIONAL filter for the registry type has been considered. The result is silent default-application: filters the assistant didn't think to surface get applied (or not applied) based on whatever the assistant remembers at the moment, and the PI cannot catch what isn't shown. Concrete instance (2026-05-24): NCDB Phase 1 for Esophageal Organ-Preservation included filters on histology, year, age, M-stage, T-stage, N-stage, treatment combinations, and surgical class — but NOT on Sequence Number (NCDB's first-primary indicator). Default behavior was 'include all primaries' (i.e., each cancer record analyzed independently, even if the patient had prior cancers). SEER Phase 1 in the same project, run as a confirmation cohort weeks later, defaulted to the opposite — restrict to 'first primary only' — because SEER convention typically applies that filter. Neither default was an explicit PI decision; both were silent assistant choices. The result was a 16,000-patient methodological gap between cohorts that was not caught until the PI questioned the SEER N. This failure is invisible to L048 (which gates literature recon, not cohort definition) and L049 (which gates synthesis-prose claims, not design decisions). It requires its own hard gate at Phase 1.1.
lessons.action: At /analyze Phase 1.1 dataset_spec.json generation, BEFORE writing any filter to the spec, the assistant MUST: (1) Identify the registry from study_spec.dataset_type. (2) Load the registry-appropriate checklist from skills/references/registry-cohort-checklists.md (NCDB has 26 standard items, SEER has 26, NSQIP has 10, UNOS has 10, TriNetX has 7, generic 5). (3) Present the checklist as a structured table with columns: filter name | common defaults in literature | proposed value for this study with rationale | [ ] PI checkbox. (4) PI selects yes / no / custom for each item; custom values require free-text rationale. (5) PI must explicitly tick 'I have reviewed every item; no filter is silently defaulted' before dataset_spec.json is written. (6) For cross-registry studies (e.g., NCDB + SEER for replication), present a side-by-side comparison table; every filter that differs between registries surfaces as a §HALT/AMBIGUITY note requiring justification. (7) The completed checklist (filters considered AND rejected) is appended to Reports/phase1_consort_<date>.md as a permanent record — not just the filters that survived. (8) When the same study uses multiple registries, deviation between registries must be either justified or eliminated by aligning filters across registries. Promote to: skills/internal/analyze/SKILL.md §1.1.a (hard-gate requirement); skills/references/registry-cohort-checklists.md (the actual checklists); CHANGELOG.md v3.6.0. Worked example: Esophageal-Organ-Preservation/Reports/seer_filter_log.json + decision_log.md 2026-05-24 entry documenting the NCDB-vs-SEER first-primary mismatch and corrective re-run.

lessons.lesson: The /analyze skill workflow had six interrelated ambiguities that were resolved together in the 2026-05-28 session. (1) PRIMARY vs SECONDARY TERMINOLOGY. 'Primary analysis' means crude/unadjusted relationship between exposure and outcome using the appropriate statistical test by outcome class (chi-square or Fisher for categorical; t-test or Wilcoxon for continuous; log-rank + univariable Cox for time-to-event; chi-square + crude OR for cross-sectional binary). 'Secondary analysis' means PSM + multivariable adjusted models + KM survival curves + IPTW + method variants (GBT-IPTW, AIPW, frailty Cox). The prior skill called the adjusted multivariable model 'primary,' which is incorrect under standard biostatistical convention and confuses the workflow boundary between Phase 4 (where no adjustment is allowed) and Phase 5 (where adjustment lives). (2) OBJECTIVES LOCK at Phase 1.0. The primary objective (single contrast sentence: population, exposure, primary outcome, time horizon, comparator) and numbered secondary_objectives[] (each with their own outcome/exposure/time-horizon/comparator) are locked separately from the analysis plan and saved to specs/objectives_locked.json + Protocol/objectives_locked_<date>.md. Immutable after PI sign-off. Any change is a dated SAP §9-style amendment in Protocol/sap_amendments.md, never a silent edit. This prevents post-hoc objective drift; everything downstream (Excel workbook tabs, analysis plan, HALT presentations) is scoped to these objectives. (3) MASTER EXCEL WORKBOOK at Phase 1.3. Replaces the legacy markdown table_layouts.md with a single production-grade Excel file Reports/MASTER_TABLES_<project>_<date>.xlsx carrying named tabs (Table_1, Table_2, Table_3, Sensitivity, Supplementary_1, Supplementary_2, ...). Coexists with MASTER_ANALYSIS_REGISTRY.json (per L045 — machine truth with history[]) and its auto-rendered .md index. Three-artifact role split: JSON = machine audit trail with supersede history; Excel = human-facing tabular manuscript source (formatted, bolded); MD = quick scannable human view. (4) MATCHING vs ADJUSTMENT VARIABLES SEPARATED. Phase 2 analysis_plan.json holds matching_variables[] (the design dimension) distinct from adjustment_covariates[] (the estimation dimension). Each entry has its own rationale (DAG, clinical relevance, comparator paper precedent, missingness profile). Overlap allowed but not required. (5) HALT 2A. A new hard-stop halt between Phase 4 and Phase 5. PI is presented with a structured table per locked objective showing each candidate variable with Match? Adjust? Rationale columns; PI selects yes/no per variable per role; custom additions require free-text rationale; PI must explicitly tick 'I have reviewed every variable; no variable is silently included or excluded' before sign-off. On sign-off: write Protocol/variables_locked_<date>.md + specs/variables_locked.json. Phase 5 reads specs/variables_locked.json at start and halts immediately if absent or unsigned. (6) BOLDING RULE. In the Excel workbook, bold every cell in Table_1 where p<0.05 (Phase 4 crude/unadjusted threshold); bold every cell in Table_2/Sensitivity/Supplementary_* where BH-FDR q<0.05 (Phase 5 adjusted rigor-gate threshold). Cells where p<0.05 but q>=0.05 are NOT bolded — this distinguishes raw from FDR significance for the PI scanner. (7) SESSION-END 4-ARTIFACT RECONCILIATION. At every session-end, for every project with both abstract.docx and manuscript.docx, cross-walk every numeric value across abstract ↔ manuscript ↔ Excel ↔ JSON at 3-decimal precision. Per L042, scan BOTH manuscript paragraphs AND embedded table cells (python-docx Document.paragraphs excludes table-cell text — the canonical bug where prose updates leave stale Table values). Output Reports/session_end_reconciliation_<date>.md with PASS/FAIL/DRIFT per claim. Any FAIL or DRIFT must be resolved or logged as an explicit open blocker before session close; never silently passed. Companion implementation in manuscript-qc Check 16.
